title: World Education Project
text:
The value of this group project to me

Technical skills:

deploying a Shiny dashboard to share insights interactively on Posit Cloud, a great substitute if Tableau or Power BI are not available.
applying data cleaning, EDA, visualization, and dashboarding techniques.

Collaboration:

working with a team of 4 data science students to divide tasks, share insights, and integrate work into a cohesive project.

Project management:

coordinating timelines, version control, and communication to ensure smooth progress.





Overview
This project analyzes global education indicators to explore patterns in enrollment, attainment, and inequality across countries. The analysis was completed as part of UBC Master of Data Science, DSCI 532 Data Visualization course and demonstrates data cleaning, exploratory analysis, visualization, and basic modeling to surface insights relevant to education gaps between genders and international regions. Then finally, deploy the insights in an interactive dashboard built with Shiny, and a chatbot interface using a large language model to query the data.


Project repository
Forked project repo: World Education Project



Data
Primary files (from the repo)

data/processed/processed_global_education.csv: cleaned, merged dataset used for analysis.

data/raw/Global_Education.csv: raw source files sourced from Kaggle - World Educational Data, compiled from UNESCO Institute for Statistics, UNICEF, and UN Statistics Division.
notebooks/EDA.ipynb: analysis jupyter notebooks (Python) used to produce figures and tables.




Methods & Analytics

Data cleaning & integration: harmonized country codes, handled missingness, merged multiple sources.

Exploratory data analysis: summary statistics, distributions, and correlation matrices.
Segmentation: country clustering by region, gender, education outcomes and socioeconomic indicators.
Visualization: maps, comparative bar charts, correlation scatter plot.
Chatbot using LLM: used a large language model to automatically filter and explore the data.




Dashboard
Link to the dashboard
We built an interactive dashboard using Shiny app to allow users to explore the data and insights dynamically. The dashboard includes: World map views with metric selector, bar charts comparing average education level by region, and completion rate gap by region, scatter plot comparing male and female literacy rate by region, and a chatbot interface for querying the data using natural language.

Landing page 
Interactive plots 
Chatbot page

title: SQL query optimization study
text: A structured benchmarking study comparing common SQL patterns – CTEs vs subqueries, window functions vs GROUP BY aggregations – on large datasets in both PostgreSQL 16 and BigQuery.

title: SQL query optimization study
text: A structured benchmarking study comparing common SQL patterns – CTEs vs subqueries, window functions vs GROUP BY aggregations – on large datasets in both PostgreSQL 16 and BigQuery.

title: SQL query optimization study
text:
Motivation
Much SQL advice online is untested or dialect-specific. I wanted concrete, reproducible numbers for the patterns I use most often.

title: SQL query optimization study
text:
Method

Generated synthetic datasets of 10M rows using Python’s faker library
Ran each query 10 times and recorded median wall time
Controlled for caching by running DISCARD ALL between trials in Postgres

title: SQL query optimization study
text:
Selected findings



Pattern
PostgreSQL
BigQuery




CTE (non-recursive)
1.2s
0.8s


Equivalent subquery
1.1s
0.9s


Window ROW_NUMBER()
0.7s
0.4s


GROUP BY + join
1.4s
0.6s



CTEs in PostgreSQL are not optimization fences as of v12+, which surprised many colleagues who avoided them for performance reasons.

title: SQL query optimization study
text:
Code
SELECT
  user_id,
  event_date,
  SUM(revenue) OVER (
    PARTITION BY user_id
    ORDER BY event_date
    ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
  ) AS cumulative_revenue
FROM events;

title: Species name search
text: This project builds a production-grade species name search tool that aggregates results from over 15 external biodiversity databases including GBIF and Symbiota portals.

title: Species name search
text: This project builds a production-grade species name search tool that aggregates results from over 15 external biodiversity databases including GBIF and Symbiota portals.

title: Species name search
text:
Problem
Biodiversity researchers often need to reconcile species names across multiple taxonomic authorities. No single API covers all databases, and sequential requests are too slow for interactive use.

title: Species name search
text:
Approach

Async fan-out: Used httpx with asyncio.gather to query all APIs concurrently, reducing median latency from ~8s sequential to ~1.2s.
Redis caching: Common species names are cached with a 24-hour TTL, cutting repeat query cost to near zero.
PostgreSQL: Stores canonical name mappings and user search history.
Next.js frontend: Displays ranked results with source attribution and fuzzy match scores.

title: Species name search
text:
Key code
import asyncio
import httpx

async def fan_out(name: str, sources: list[str]) -> dict:
    async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=5.0) as client:
        tasks = [query_source(client, name, src) for src in sources]
        results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks, return_exceptions=True)
    return {src: r for src, r in zip(sources, results) if not isinstance(r, Exception)}

title: Species name search
text:
Results

Sub-2s response time across 15 sources
94% cache hit rate on common queries after first week
Deployed on a single $6/month VPS with no downtime in 3 months

title: Module 4: Query Optimization
text: Practical techniques for writing faster, more efficient SQL queries.

title: Module 4: Query Optimization
text:
Use Indexes

Add indexes on columns you frequently filter or join on
Avoid full table scans on large datasets

CREATE INDEX idx_orders_status ON orders(status);

title: Module 4: Query Optimization
text:
Push Filters Early
-- Slow: joins first, then filters
SELECT * FROM orders
JOIN customers ON orders.customer_id = customers.id
WHERE orders.status = 'completed';

-- Fast: filter before join
SELECT o.id, c.name
FROM (SELECT * FROM orders WHERE status = 'completed') o
JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.id;

Python handles what you do with it
Together they cover the full pipeline: extract → transform → analyze → visualize

title: Data Science with Python & SQL
text:
Data Wrangling with pandas
import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_csv("data.csv")

# Filter, group, aggregate
result = (
    df[df["year"] == 2024]
    .groupby("category")["revenue"]
    .sum()

title: Data Science with Python & SQL
text:
Query Optimization Basics

Use indexes on columns you filter or join on
Avoid SELECT * — only fetch what you need
Push filters early, before joins
Use EXPLAIN to inspect query plans

-- Before
SELECT * FROM orders JOIN customers ON orders.customer_id = customers.id;

-- After
SELECT orders.id, customers.name
FROM orders
JOIN customers ON orders.customer_id = customers.id
WHERE orders.status = 'completed';

title: Data Science with Python & SQL
text:
Visualization Tips

Match chart type to your question

Distribution → histogram or boxplot
Trend → line chart
Comparison → bar chart

Label axes clearly
Avoid chartjunk

title: When to use DuckDB vs pandas for local analytics
text: Both DuckDB and pandas can handle local analytical workloads, but they have very different performance profiles. After hitting pandas memory limits on a 5M-row CSV, I did a side-by-side comparison.

title: When to use DuckDB vs pandas for local analytics
text: Both DuckDB and pandas can handle local analytical workloads, but they have very different performance profiles. After hitting pandas memory limits on a 5M-row CSV, I did a side-by-side comparison.

title: When to use DuckDB vs pandas for local analytics
text:
The short answer
Use DuckDB when:

Your dataset is larger than ~500MB
You are doing aggregations, joins, or window functions
You want to query Parquet or CSV files without loading them into memory

Use pandas when:

You need row-wise Python logic (.apply, custom functions)
You are doing iterative data cleaning with lots of intermediate steps
The dataset fits comfortably in memory and transformation is simple

title: When to use DuckDB vs pandas for local analytics
text:
Benchmark
import duckdb
import pandas as pd
import time

# DuckDB aggregation
start = time.perf_counter()
duckdb.sql("SELECT category, AVG(value) FROM 'data.parquet' GROUP BY category").df()
print(f"DuckDB: {time.perf_counter() - start:.2f}s")

# pandas equivalent
start = time.perf_counter()
df = pd.read_parquet("data.parquet")
df.groupby("category")["value"].mean()
print(f"pandas: {time.perf_counter() - start:.2f}s")
On 5M rows: DuckDB finished in 0.3s, pandas in 2.1s – and pandas used 1.4GB RAM vs DuckDB’s 180MB.

title: When to use DuckDB vs pandas for local analytics
text:
Takeaway
DuckDB is not a replacement for pandas. They compose well: use DuckDB to filter and aggregate down to a manageable size, then hand off to pandas for Python-native transformations.

title: Data with Will
text:
繁體中文 | 简体中文
Greetings! My name is William Song, a data scientist and analyst based in Vancouver, BC, Canada.
My work sits at the intersection of business analytics, statistical modeling, data engineering, and visualization.
Like many of you, AI (LLMs in particular) has impacted my workflow in profound ways, and I am excited about the possibilities it opens up for data professionals. Also, I am aware of the potential risks it poses, technical-wise (security risk) and skill-wise (skill atrophy). That said, I also continue to write raw SQL and Python code from time to time to practice and to solve problems from the ground up.

title: Washrooms in Vancouver Parks
text:
The value of this group project to me

Technical skills:

creating a containerized environment using Docker to practice a reproducible workflow.
applying data cleaning, EDA, machine learning models (baseline, KNN, SVM RBF), and visualization techniques.

Collaboration:

working with a team of 3 data science students to divide tasks, share insights, and integrate work into a cohesive project.

Project management:

coordinating timelines, version control, and communication to ensure smooth progress.





Overview
This project maps and analyzes public washroom availability in Vancouver parks. It combines spatial data, park metadata, and accessibility attributes to identify coverage gaps and inform planning decisions for park amenities. The analysis was completed as part of UBC Master of Data Science, DSCI 522 Data Science Workflow course.


Project repository
Forked project repo: washrooms-in-vancouver-parks



Report
The final report can be found here: Predicting the Presence of Washrooms in Vancouver Parks.



Data

Source and scope

City of Vancouver Parks dataset (Vancouver 2025) covering 218 parks with facility attributes, locations, and sizes.



Key variables used

Target: washrooms (presence/absence).
Predictors: park size (hectares), neighbourhood, official park flag, advisories, counts of additional facilities, and special features.



Notes and limitations

The dataset is cross‑sectional and may omit contextual features (visitor counts, funding cycles) that could improve predictive performance.





Methods & Analytics

Exploratory analysis

Compare distributions of binary amenities and park size between parks with and without washrooms (frequency plots, histograms).



Modeling approach

Baseline: Dummy classifier to set a reference accuracy.
Models tested: K‑Nearest Neighbors (KNN) and Support Vector Machine with RBF kernel (SVM RBF).
Validation: 70/30 train/test split with 5‑fold cross‑validation on training data.
Preprocessing: Standardize numeric features; encode categorical predictors (neighbourhood, flags); handle missingness prior to fitting.



Evaluation metrics

Cross‑validation test score, training score, and confusion matrix on held‑out test set to assess generalization and class balance.





Key Findings

Parks with washrooms tend to be larger: median hectare is higher for parks that include washrooms.
Washroom presence correlates with other amenities: parks offering more facilities are more likely to have washrooms.
SVM RBF outperformed KNN and the baseline: in cross‑validation, though the improvement over the dummy baseline was modest, indicating limited predictive signal in the chosen features.
KNN showed signs of overfitting: (higher training score, lower test performance) and therefore generalized poorly.
Implication: richer features (e.g., visitor counts, park usage, funding/prioritization data) or alternative modeling strategies are likely needed to produce stronger, actionable predictions.




Visualizations

Model comparison: 
Confusion matrix:
